title: Natural-language processing

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Natural-language processing; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.claim: NLP tasks include tokenization, representation, classification, extraction, retrieval, translation, and generation; evaluation must match the task, language varieties, stakeholders, and error costs.

sources.claim: NLP tasks include tokenization, representation, classification, extraction, retrieval, translation, and generation; evaluation must match the task, language varieties, stakeholders, and error costs.

canonicalExplanation.summary: NLP tasks include tokenization, representation, classification, extraction, retrieval, translation, and generation; evaluation must match the task, language varieties, stakeholders, and error costs.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A support classifier reports per-language and dialect confusion metrics against a keyword baseline and sends uncertain high-cost cases to review. Your goal is to frame and evaluate a small NLP task while identifying language and dialect coverage gaps. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Neural networks and learned representations; Knowledge representation and uncertainty; Convolution, sequence and attention roles; Problem, target and success measure. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: NLP tasks include tokenization, representation, classification, extraction, retrieval, translation, and generation; evaluation must match the task, language varieties, stakeholders, and error costs. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Tokens are model units not words, benchmark language coverage is limited, automatic overlap metrics miss meaning and harms, dialect performance varies, generated text can be unsupported, and annotations encode policy. The learner must reject this overclaim: One English benchmark or text-overlap metric establishes that an NLP system understands language and works for all dialects.

includes: Distinguish tokenization, representation, classification, extraction, translation and generation tasks.
Frame and evaluate a small NLP task while identifying language and dialect coverage gaps.
excludes: Tokens are model units not words, benchmark language coverage is limited, automatic overlap metrics miss meaning and harms, dialect performance varies, generated text can be unsupported, and annotations encode policy.

outcomes: Frame and evaluate a small NLP task while identifying language and dialect coverage gaps.

examples.title: Natural-language processing: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A support classifier reports per-language and dialect confusion metrics against a keyword baseline and sends uncertain high-cost cases to review.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A support classifier reports per-language and dialect confusion metrics against a keyword baseline and sends uncertain high-cost cases to review.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Frame the exact language task and population, evaluate task-specific errors and coverage slices, and include qualified human review where metrics miss meaning or harm.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Frame the exact language task and population, evaluate task-specific errors and coverage slices, and include qualified human review where metrics miss meaning or harm.

examples.title: Natural-language processing: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: A translation system with a good average score mistranslates negation in one domain, so targeted human evaluation blocks deployment there.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: A translation system with a good average score mistranslates negation in one domain, so targeted human evaluation blocks deployment there.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Tokens are model units not words, benchmark language coverage is limited, automatic overlap metrics miss meaning and harms, dialect performance varies, generated text can be unsupported, and annotations encode policy.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: One English benchmark or text-overlap metric establishes that an NLP system understands language and works for all dialects.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Frame and evaluate a small NLP task while identifying language and dialect coverage gaps.

limitations: more modalities or fluent outputs do not establish understanding, truth, physical safety, or universal coverage

recap.summary: You should now be able to frame and evaluate a small NLP task while identifying language and dialect coverage gaps. Keep this boundary in view: Tokens are model units not words, benchmark language coverage is limited, automatic overlap metrics miss meaning and harms, dialect performance varies, generated text can be unsupported, and annotations encode policy.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Natural-language processing with one technical mechanism, one evidence requirement, and one non-claim.

Apply the boundary to: A translation system with a good average score mistranslates negation in one domain, so targeted human evaluation blocks deployment there.
